verses.text: Then David said, This is the house of the LORD God, and this is the altar of the burnt offering for Israel. Guidance: Standing at Ornan's threshingfloor, where God had answered him by fire and stayed the destroying angel, David makes a momentous declaration: 'This is the house of the LORD God, and this is the altar of the burnt offering for Israel.' Here, on this very spot — Mount Moriah, where Abraham had once offered Isaac and God provided a substitute (Gen 22) — the temple would be built. The chapter is showing us how God chose the site of His house: not by David's strategy or convenience, but at the place where judgment was met by mercy through sacrifice. The chapter is teaching us that the dwelling place of God among His people is established on the ground of atonement; the temple would rise where the sword had been sheathed because blood was shed and fire fell. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD turns the place of our deepest humbling into the place of His richest blessing — the threshingfloor of judgment becomes the temple of worship. The chapter is teaching us, too, the discernment of faith: David recognized God's hand and God's choice, and declared it plainly, settling the matter for all the generations to come. Where God had so signally answered, there His house must be. And for the Christian, this spot of atonement, this Moriah of substitution and provided sacrifice, points unfailingly to the place where the true and final sacrifice would be offered — to Christ, in whom God and sinners meet, the true temple and the true altar, where judgment and mercy embrace forever. The chapter is teaching us that all true worship is founded on the place of sacrifice, and that the house of God is built where atonement has been made.

And David commanded to gather together the strangers that were in the land of Israel; and he set masons to hew wrought stones to build the house of God. And David prepared iron in abundance for the nails for the doors of the gates, and for the joinings; and brass in abundance without weight; Also cedar trees in abundance: for the Zidonians and they of Tyre brought much cedar wood to David. And David said, Solomon my son is young and tender, and the house that is to be builded for the LORD must be exceeding magnifical, of fame and of glory throughout all countries: I will therefore now make preparation for it. So David prepared abundantly before his death. Guidance: Though David himself would not build the temple, he throws himself into preparing for it with all his heart — gathering workmen, hewing stones, laying up iron, brass 'without weight,' and abundant cedar. His reasoning is touching: 'Solomon my son is young and tender, and the house... must be exceeding magnifical... I will therefore now make preparation for it. So David prepared abundantly before his death.' The chapter is showing us a man who labours wholeheartedly for a work he will not live to complete or even to build — selflessly preparing so that his son might succeed. The chapter is teaching us the grace of preparing for those who come after us; David did not say, 'If I cannot build it, let Solomon gather his own materials,' but spent his last years amassing everything his son would need. We are taught to work not only for our own day but for the generations to follow, to lay up for our children and successors the means to do the work of God. The chapter is teaching us that the house of God deserves our best — 'exceeding magnifical, of fame and of glory throughout all countries'; David spared no expense or effort, for nothing is too good for the LORD. The chapter is teaching us, too, a tender wisdom in providing for the young and inexperienced: knowing Solomon was 'young and tender,' David eased his path by preparing abundantly beforehand. So godly parents and leaders smooth the way for those they will leave behind. The chapter is teaching us to give ourselves fully to God's work even when we will not see its finishing, content to be those who prepare and gather, trusting others to build — for the kingdom of God is the labour of many hands across many generations, and the faithful preparer is as needful as the builder.

Then he called for Solomon his son, and charged him to build an house for the LORD God of Israel. And David said to Solomon, My son, as for me, it was in my mind to build an house unto the name of the LORD my God: But the word of the LORD came to me, saying, Thou hast shed blood abundantly, and hast made great wars: thou shalt not build an house unto my name, because thou hast shed much blood upon the earth in my sight. Behold, a son shall be born to thee, who shall be a man of rest; and I will give him rest from all his enemies round about: for his name shall be Solomon, and I will give peace and quietness unto Israel in his days. He shall build an house for my name; and he shall be my son, and I will be his father; and I will establish the throne of his kingdom over Israel for ever. Guidance: David explains to Solomon why the building of the temple has fallen to him and not to his father: 'Thou hast shed blood abundantly, and hast made great wars: thou shalt not build an house unto my name.' The temple, the house of the God of peace, was to be built not by the man of war but by Solomon, 'a man of rest,' whose very name means peace, to whom God would give 'peace and quietness.' The chapter is showing us the fitness of God's appointments: the house of worship was to be built in a time of rest, by a man of peace, for the LORD has His own wisdom in assigning every work to its proper hand and season. The chapter is teaching us that God does not always entrust His most sacred works to His most prominent servants; David, the great king and warrior, the man after God's own heart, was set aside from this honour because of the blood of his wars, while the quieter Solomon was chosen for it. We are taught to accept God's distribution of tasks without envy or complaint; David did not resent being barred from building but gladly prepared for his son to do it. The chapter is teaching us, in the name and character of Solomon — the man of rest, the prince of peace — a beautiful foreshadowing of Christ, the true Son of David and Prince of Peace, who builds the real temple (His church) not by war and bloodshed but by His own peace-making sacrifice, and whose kingdom God has established 'for ever.' The covenant words 'he shall be my son, and I will be his father' reach beyond Solomon to Christ (as Hebrews shows), and the everlasting throne is His. The chapter is teaching us that the building of God's true house belongs to the Prince of Peace, and that all our labours find their meaning in Him whose kingdom shall never end.

Now, my son, the LORD be with thee; and prosper thou, and build the house of the LORD thy God, as he hath said of thee. Only the LORD give thee wisdom and understanding, and give thee charge concerning Israel, that thou mayest keep the law of the LORD thy God. Then shalt thou prosper, if thou takest heed to fulfil the statutes and judgments which the LORD charged Moses with concerning Israel: be strong, and of good courage; dread not, nor be dismayed. Now, behold, in my trouble I have prepared for the house of the LORD an hundred thousand talents of gold, and a thousand thousand talents of silver; and of brass and iron without weight; for it is in abundance: timber also and stone have I prepared; and thou mayest add thereto. Moreover there are workmen with thee in abundance, hewers and workers of stone and timber, and all manner of cunning men for every manner of work. Of the gold, the silver, and the brass, and the iron, there is no number. Arise therefore, and be doing, and the LORD be with thee. Guidance: David gives Solomon a stirring charge, full of faith and fatherly love: 'the LORD be with thee; and prosper thou, and build the house of the LORD thy God... be strong, and of good courage; dread not, nor be dismayed... Arise therefore, and be doing, and the LORD be with thee.' He tells him the secret of true prosperity — not the vast treasures laid up (though these were immense, gold and silver beyond number) but obedience: 'Then shalt thou prosper, if thou takest heed to fulfil the statutes and judgments... of the LORD.' The chapter is showing us the heart of a godly father passing on both the work and the wisdom to his son — equipping him with materials, but above all with the charge to keep God's law and the assurance of God's presence. The chapter is teaching us that prosperity in God's service flows from obedience to God's word; all the gold in the world cannot make the work succeed apart from faithfulness to the LORD's commands. The chapter is teaching us the great encouragement for every daunting task: 'be strong, and of good courage; dread not, nor be dismayed' — the same words God gave Joshua (Josh 1), and the same word we need for whatever work God sets before us. We are not to be paralyzed by the greatness of the task or our own weakness, for 'the LORD be with thee' is the promise that emboldens us. The chapter is teaching us the call to action: 'Arise therefore, and be doing' — faith does not sit idle but rises and works, trusting that God goes with the obedient. The chapter is teaching us to combine diligent labour ('be doing') with humble dependence ('the LORD be with thee') and steadfast obedience ('keep the law'). Here is the recipe for prospering in any work for God: take courage, keep His word, rise and do the task, and rest in His presence — for the LORD is with those who go forward in faith to do His will.

The chapter is teaching us the great encouragement for every daunting task: 'be strong, and of good courage; dread not, nor be dismayed' — the same words God gave Joshua (Josh 1), and the same word we need for whatever work God sets before us. We are not to be paralyzed by the greatness of the task or our own weakness, for 'the LORD be with thee' is the promise that emboldens us. The chapter is teaching us the call to action: 'Arise therefore, and be doing' — faith does not sit idle but rises and works, trusting that God goes with the obedient. The chapter is teaching us to combine diligent labour ('be doing') with humble dependence ('the LORD be with thee') and steadfast obedience ('keep the law'). Here is the recipe for prospering in any work for God: take courage, keep His word, rise and do the task, and rest in His presence — for the LORD is with those who go forward in faith to do His will.

Now, my son, the LORD be with thee; and prosper thou, and build the house of the LORD thy God, as he hath said of thee. Only the LORD give thee wisdom and understanding, and give thee charge concerning Israel, that thou mayest keep the law of the LORD thy God. Then shalt thou prosper, if thou takest heed to fulfil the statutes and judgments which the LORD charged Moses with concerning Israel: be strong, and of good courage; dread not, nor be dismayed. Now, behold, in my trouble I have prepared for the house of the LORD an hundred thousand talents of gold, and a thousand thousand talents of silver; and of brass and iron without weight; for it is in abundance: timber also and stone have I prepared; and thou mayest add thereto. Moreover there are workmen with thee in abundance, hewers and workers of stone and timber, and all manner of cunning men for every manner of work. Of the gold, the silver, and the brass, and the iron, there is no number. Arise therefore, and be doing, and the LORD be with thee. Guidance: David gives Solomon a stirring charge, full of faith and fatherly love: 'the LORD be with thee; and prosper thou, and build the house of the LORD thy God... be strong, and of good courage; dread not, nor be dismayed... Arise therefore, and be doing, and the LORD be with thee.' He tells him the secret of true prosperity — not the vast treasures laid up (though these were immense, gold and silver beyond number) but obedience: 'Then shalt thou prosper, if thou takest heed to fulfil the statutes and judgments... of the LORD.' The chapter is showing us the heart of a godly father passing on both the work and the wisdom to his son — equipping him with materials, but above all with the charge to keep God's law and the assurance of God's presence. The chapter is teaching us that prosperity in God's service flows from obedience to God's word; all the gold in the world cannot make the work succeed apart from faithfulness to the LORD's commands. The chapter is teaching us the great encouragement for every daunting task: 'be strong, and of good courage; dread not, nor be dismayed' — the same words God gave Joshua (Josh 1), and the same word we need for whatever work God sets before us. We are not to be paralyzed by the greatness of the task or our own weakness, for 'the LORD be with thee' is the promise that emboldens us. The chapter is teaching us the call to action: 'Arise therefore, and be doing' — faith does not sit idle but rises and works, trusting that God goes with the obedient. The chapter is teaching us to combine diligent labour ('be doing') with humble dependence ('the LORD be with thee') and steadfast obedience ('keep the law'). Here is the recipe for prospering in any work for God: take courage, keep His word, rise and do the task, and rest in His presence — for the LORD is with those who go forward in faith to do His will.

The chapter is teaching us the call to action: 'Arise therefore, and be doing' — faith does not sit idle but rises and works, trusting that God goes with the obedient. The chapter is teaching us to combine diligent labour ('be doing') with humble dependence ('the LORD be with thee') and steadfast obedience ('keep the law'). Here is the recipe for prospering in any work for God: take courage, keep His word, rise and do the task, and rest in His presence — for the LORD is with those who go forward in faith to do His will.

David also commanded all the princes of Israel to help Solomon his son, saying, Is not the LORD your God with you? and hath he not given you rest on every side? for he hath given the inhabitants of the land into mine hand; and the land is subdued before the LORD, and before his people. Now set your heart and your soul to seek the LORD your God; arise therefore, and build ye the sanctuary of the LORD God, to bring the ark of the covenant of the LORD, and the holy vessels of God, into the house that is to be built to the name of the LORD. Guidance: David turns from his son to all the princes of Israel and enlists their help, grounding his appeal in what God has already done: 'Is not the LORD your God with you? and hath he not given you rest on every side?' Because God has given them peace and subdued the land, they are now to throw themselves into the great work — and David's central charge is unforgettable: 'Now set your heart and your soul to seek the LORD your God; arise therefore, and build ye the sanctuary.' The chapter is showing us that the work of God is not for the king and his heir alone but for all the people; David summons the leaders to share in building the LORD's house. The chapter is teaching us that the great motive for serving God is the remembrance of His goodness: 'Is not the LORD your God with you?' — He has been with them, given them rest, subdued their enemies, and therefore they owe Him their wholehearted devotion. Gratitude for past mercy fuels present obedience. The chapter is teaching us the very heart of true religion, the refrain that echoes all through Chronicles: 'Set your heart and your soul to seek the LORD your God.' Not half-hearted, not divided, but with heart and soul — this is the seeking God desires, the seeking that Saul refused and David lived. The chapter is teaching us that seeking the LORD and building His house belong together: those who set their hearts to seek God will arise and labour for His worship and His glory. The chapter is teaching us to be like these princes — moved by God's goodness, seeking Him with whole heart and soul, and rising to take our part in the building of His sanctuary. For the people of God in every age, the call stands: remember what the LORD has done, set your whole heart to seek Him, and arise to build His house — that His presence, symbolized by the ark, may dwell among His people, and His name be glorified.
